transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article objectively and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words, neutral tone)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items — the 5 most objectively significant facts
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective or source type included
- Do not editorialize, adopt the article's framing, or use loaded language
- Represent all perspectives present in the article proportionally

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S
SOURCE: Newsweek

ARTICLE:

By Ellie CookSenior Defense ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberU.S. President Donald Trump has said the U.S. is "seeking immediate negotiations" to acquire Greenland, deepening the yawning chasm opening up between Washington and its closest allies over the Arctic territory.Trump called Greenland "part of North America" and "our territory" during a rambling speech to world leaders at the World Economic Forum in the Swiss town of Davos on Wednesday.The Republican reiterated his claim that the U.S. is the only power that can protect the mostly ice-covered expanse, and insisted this would not pose a threat to NATO.Alliance officials have repeatedly said a U.S. military attack on Greenland, a semi-autonomous part of Denmark, would spell the end of NATO. The prospect of the U.S. — the most influential and powerful member of NATO —attacking territory belonging to a fellow alliance member has always been unfathomable, until now.The White House had refused to rule out using military force to take Greenland, which would be a violation of international law."I don't want to use force. I won't use force," Trump said. "All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland."This is a breaking story. Newsweek Contributors Debate6 min readOpinionSyria's Kurds Lose Out | Opinion6 min readOpinionAgriculture Secretary: A Year of Results—How the Trump Admin Put American Farmers First | Opinion6 min readOpinionThe New American ICE Age | Opinion5 min readFor MembersOpinionConventional Wisdom: Greenland Edition3 min read
---

  "summary": "U.S. President Donald Trump announced at the World Economic Forum in Davos that the United States is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, escalating tensions with NATO allies. Trump described Greenland as "part of North America" and "our territory," claiming only the U.S. can protect the Arctic territory. While stating he does not want to use military force, the White House had previously refused to rule out that option. NATO officials have warned that any U.S. military action against Greenland, a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark, would end the alliance. The prospect of the U.S. attacking territory belonging to a NATO member represents an unprecedented challenge to the alliance. Trump insisted his pursuit of Greenland would not threaten NATO, though using military force would violate international law. parsed_output.summary: U.S. President Donald Trump announced at the World Economic Forum in Davos that the United States is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland, escalating tensions with NATO allies. Trump described Greenland as "part of North America" and "our territory," claiming only the U.S. can protect the Arctic territory. While stating he does not want to use military force, the White House had previously refused to rule out that option. NATO officials have warned that any U.S. military action against Greenland, a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark, would end the alliance. The prospect of the U.S. attacking territory belonging to a NATO member represents an unprecedented challenge to the alliance. Trump insisted his pursuit of Greenland would not threaten NATO, though using military force would violate international law. The situation highlights growing friction between Washington and its traditional European allies over Arctic territorial claims.
key_facts: Trump announced the U.S. is seeking immediate negotiations to acquire Greenland during a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos
Greenland is a semi-autonomous territory of Denmark, a NATO member state
Trump stated he will not use military force, though the White House had previously refused to rule out that option
NATO officials have warned that a U.S. military attack on Greenland would end the NATO alliance
Using military force to take Greenland would violate international law
